abstract.text: This article describes common issues that may arise when using RFIDManager.

content.text: 1. Does the SDK support Objective-C, and is there Objective-C documentation?

inlineContent.text: The SDK is developed in Swift, but it also supports being called from Objective-C.
DocC supports Objective-C, but the statically hosted documentation generated by DocC only includes Swift interfaces. Objective-C content is not displayed on the static website.
Objective-C content can be viewed on macOS.

content.text: 2. Can the SDK be called from multiple threads? Does it support sending commands concurrently?

inlineContent.text: It can be called from background threads, but it does not support sending commands concurrently.
Most methods in the SDK are blocking calls, so it is recommended to execute them on a background thread to avoid blocking the main thread.
Please note that the device module can process only one command at a time, so do not send multiple commands simultaneously from different threads.
It is recommended to call multiple commands sequentially on the same background thread rather than invoking them concurrently from multiple threads.

content.text: 3. Why is RFID tag data usually displayed in hexadecimal? Can ASCII characters be stored?

inlineContent.text: In the SDK, tag data is usually displayed or transmitted as hexadecimal strings to make low-level data easier to view and process.
To store ASCII characters, the string can first be converted into the corresponding hexadecimal string using ASCII encoding, and then that hexadecimal string can be written to the RFID tag.
After the tag data is read or inventoried, the hexadecimal data can be converted back into an ASCII string, thereby enabling the storage and reading of ASCII content.

content.text: 4. Why does a “Bluetooth Pairing Request” pop-up appear when connecting over Bluetooth? Should Pair or Cancel be selected? Can this pop-up be disabled?

inlineContent.text: This pop-up is displayed by iOS. Because the device includes a keyboard service, iOS recognizes it as a keyboard and attempts to pair with it directly so it can reconnect automatically later.

For this pop-up, it is recommended to tap Cancel. If Pair is selected, the device will establish a connection with iOS, which may cause some issues. For example, once the device is connected to the system, the app can disconnect only at the app level and cannot disconnect the device from the system itself. As long as the device is still connected to the system, it will not appear in Bluetooth scanning results because it is already in a connected state.

This pop-up can be disabled. It appears because the device exposes a keyboard service and is therefore recognized by iOS as a keyboard. To disable the pop-up, the keyboard service must be disabled. Please note that only Bluetooth version 2.10 and later supports disabling the keyboard service, and after it is disabled, the HID service will no longer be available.
To disable this pop-up, first ignore the paired device in Settings -> Bluetooth, and then perform the following steps:
Connect to the device in the app, switch to Bluetooth settings, and tap the Get button to retrieve the current Bluetooth configuration
Turn off the “Bluetooth binding attribute” and “HID keyboard service” options
Tap the Set button. After the setting is applied, the device will disconnect Bluetooth and restart, which is normal
If the pop-up still appears, reconnect to the device and tap the “Delete bonded device” button

abstract.text: This article introduces the basic concepts of RFID to help you better understand the RFIDManager library.
This tutorial will guide you through importing the RFIDManager library into your project and show you how to use it.

ble-settings.png.alt: Example of disabling the pairing pop-up in the Bluetooth settings page

abstract.text: A framework for RFID tag reading, writing, and management on Apple platforms.
